notes: An enclitic used to form a question. For enclitic see Ls 2, Gram. Note 3.
Frequently combined with "chin" to mean "very much/very many." This "chē" is the same character and meaning as the "chē" in "lōa-chē." Distinguish from "chē" of "chhiá* chē..."
If the subject is 2d person, this term is usually a greeting. "Bōe" here is enclitic.

"Kùi" will be used in a later lesson to mean "expensive". Using the honorific term "kùi" is a polite way of asking the name of an adult. It is not ordinarily spoken to younger people, never to children.
The "lâng" frequently changes tone, an exception to the rule that one-syllable nouns do not change tone.

english: no one / not one (person) / there is no one who
there is someone who... / there are people who...
clan name / family name / surname; to be surnamed

notes: The word for "wife" will be in Ls 4.

english: Sorry; Please excuse me; to be impolite
notes: = I beg your pardon for my want of politeness; a breaching of etiquette.
A religious sister; this term is not used to denote blood relationship.
A religious brother; this term is not used to denote blood relationship.

E.g., to read Scripture lesson in church; in this case we do not say "khòa*". tha̍k-chheh = to study. Used only of a student in reference to school books; by extension it means "to be a student". Distinguish from "khòa*-chheh" which means "to read a book" but not in the sense of studying.
